coredata.dc:title: Investigation of smart coating based on epoxy resin combined with nano silica fume particles, lanthanum, and green turmeric plant inhibitor against carbon steel corrosion

coredata.dc:description: This study develops a novel smart coating for carbon steel corrosion protection, combining epoxy resin with silica fume nanoparticles, lanthanum, and natural turmeric extract. The primary aim is to enhance corrosion resistance, mechanical stability, and environmental sustainability. Silica fume nanoparticles, with their high specific surface area and porous structure, significantly improve the coating's physical and chemical properties. Lanthanum imparts self-healing capabilities, enabling the coating to repair scratches or damage and prevent corrosive species penetration. Additionally, turmeric extract, a green corrosion inhibitor, reduces the corrosion rate through its antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, contributing to environmental protection. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and Potentiodynamic Polarization (PDP) tests demonstrate that coatings combining silica fume with lanthanum or turmeric extract exhibit superior corrosion resistance in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The optimal coating, comprising 0.3 g of silica fume, lanthanum, and turmeric extract integrated into epoxy resin, achieves a corrosion resistance of approximately 929 Ω cm2 and excellent self-healing properties compared to other coatings tested in the same environment. This work offers an effective, eco-friendly solution for protecting carbon steel in industrial settings, reducing corrosion-related costs and extending equipment lifespan.

head.ce:title: Investigation of smart coating based on epoxy resin combined with nano silica fume particles, lanthanum, and green turmeric plant inhibitor against carbon steel corrosion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study develops a novel smart coating for carbon steel corrosion protection, combining epoxy resin with silica fume nanoparticles, lanthanum, and natural turmeric extract. The primary aim is to enhance corrosion resistance, mechanical stability, and environmental sustainability. Silica fume nanoparticles, with their high specific surface area and porous structure, significantly improve the coating's physical and chemical properties. Lanthanum imparts self-healing capabilities, enabling the coating to repair scratches or damage and prevent corrosive species penetration. Additionally, turmeric extract, a green corrosion inhibitor, reduces the corrosion rate through its antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, contributing to environmental protection. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and Potentiodynamic Polarization (PDP) tests demonstrate that coatings combining silica fume with lanthanum or turmeric extract exhibit superior corrosion resistance in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The optimal coating, comprising 0.3 g of silica fume, lanthanum, and turmeric extract integrated into epoxy resin, achieves a corrosion resistance of approximately 929 Ω cm2 and excellent self-healing properties compared to other coatings tested in the same environment. This work offers an effective, eco-friendly solution for protecting carbon steel in industrial settings, reducing corrosion-related costs and extending equipment lifespan.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel (CS) is a vital engineering material in industrial applications due to its excellent mechanical properties, durability, and cost-effectiveness [1,2]. However, its susceptibility to corrosion in aggressive environments reduces component lifespan, increases maintenance costs, and leads to resource wastage. To mitigate corrosion, various strategies, such as coatings, material modifications, environmental adjustments, and cathodic protection, have been developed. Among these, organic and inorganic coatings are particularly effective, acting as barriers to isolate the metal substrate from corrosive species like ions and moisture [3,4]. Epoxy resin coatings are widely used due to their ease of synthesis, affordability, and anti-corrosive properties. Nevertheless, these coatings are prone to micro- and macro-damage, with inherent porosity allowing water, oxygen, and chlorides to reach the underlying metal, accelerating corrosion [5,6].

To address these limitations, smart coatings with self-healing capabilities have emerged as a promising solution. These coatings can detect environmental changes, such as mechanical damage or chemical exposure, and autonomously repair themselves to maintain protective performance without manual intervention [7]. Self-healing mechanisms often involve the incorporation of microcapsules or nanoparticles loaded with corrosion inhibitors, which release healing agents in response to triggers like mechanical rupture, pH changes, or moisture [8,9]. Mesoporous oxide nanoparticles, such as silica fume, are particularly effective carriers due to their high specific surface area, stability, and ability to encapsulate inhibitors [10,11]. However, the size, dispersion, and mechanical stability of these carriers must be optimized to ensure uniform coating performance [12].

Corrosion inhibitors enhance the protective capabilities of self-healing coatings by reducing the corrosion rate in aggressive environments. Traditional inhibitors, such as chromates, have been largely phased out due to environmental concerns, prompting research into eco-friendly alternatives [13]. Green inhibitors derived from plant extracts, such as turmeric, offer high inhibition efficiency owing to their organic compounds, including tannins, alkaloids, and aromatic molecules with polar functional groups (e.g., nitrogen, sulfur, or oxygen) [14,15]. These compounds adsorb onto metal surfaces, forming protective layers. Additionally, rare-earth elements like lanthanum have shown promise as corrosion inhibitors with self-healing properties, further enhancing coating performance [16].

While various plant extracts, such as olive leaf, thyme, and papaya seed, have been studied for corrosion inhibition [17–19], the synergistic integration of turmeric extract as a green inhibitor with lanthanum and nano silica fume particles in epoxy-based smart coatings represents a novel approach that remains largely unexplored. This study investigates the anti-corrosive and self-healing properties of epoxy-based smart coatings incorporating silica fume nanoparticles, lanthanum, and turmeric extract for carbon steel protection in a 3.5 wt% NaCl environment. The key advancements include: (i) the first reported combination of these three components, leading to synergistic enhancement of barrier and self-healing properties; (ii) exceptional corrosion inhibition efficiency of approximately 98%, far surpassing individual components; and (iii) new insights into interfacial adsorption mechanisms involving eco-friendly organic compounds and rare-earth elements, advancing surface and interface science toward sustainable, high-performance protective coatings. By developing this environmentally benign smart coating, this work contributes to reducing corrosion-related economic losses and extending equipment lifespan in harsh industrial settings.

Carbon steel (St37) plates, measuring 1 mm × 70 mm × 100 mm, were used as substrates. The chemical composition of St37 steel, primarily consisting of iron with minor amounts of carbon, manganese, and other elements, was verified to meet industrial standards. The steel surfaces were polished using silicon carbide sandpaper with grit sizes ranging from 200 to 1200 to ensure a smooth and uniform surface. Subsequently, the samples were cleaned with distilled water to remove debris, degreased with acetone to eliminate organic contaminants, and air-dried at ambient temperature using a compressed air system (Model Kaeser SX6). This preparation ensured a clean and consistent surface for coating application. Materials used for the synthesis of self-healing coatings included silica fume nanoparticles (Sigma-Aldrich), lanthanum (purity: 99.9%, Alfa Aesar), methanol (density: 0.7918 g/cm3, Merck, Germany), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%, Merck, Germany), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%, Merck), turmeric extract (prepared in Iran), sodium chloride (NaCl, 3.5 wt% solution), epoxy resin (Epon 828, Kumho P&B Chemicals, Korea), triethylenetetramine (TETA, curing agent, Kumho P&B Chemicals, Korea), toluene (molecular weight: 92.14 g/mol, Iran), and beeswax (local supplier). All chemicals were used as received without further purification, except where specified. High-purity grades were selected to minimize potential interference from impurities. The turmeric extract was freshly prepared in-house using an optimized ultrasonic-assisted method to ensure high concentrations of active curcuminoids and related phenolic compounds critical for inhibition efficiency. Key characteristics of the main inhibitor materials are as follows: Silica fume nanoparticles (Sigma-Aldrich) were mesoporous with an average particle size of ∼0.2–0.3 μm, high specific surface area (>200 m2/g), and pore volume ∼0.8–1.2 cm3/g, making them suitable carriers for inhibitor loading. Lanthanum was supplied as high-purity (99.9%) lanthanum metal/compound (Alfa Aesar), primarily in the form of La2O3 or La(OH)3 phases upon hydration, known for its corrosion inhibition and self-healing properties. The in-house prepared turmeric extract was rich in curcuminoids (primarily curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, and bisdemethoxycurcumin) and other phenolic compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitor extraction and synthesis of protective coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Turmeric extract was prepared as a green corrosion inhibitor through an optimized multi-step process (Fig. 1 ). Turmeric powder was dispersed in distilled water (1:10 w/v ratio) and ultrasonically extracted (Elmasonic S30H, 40 kHz, 50 °C, 30 min) to release active compounds. The suspension was filtered (Whatman No. 1 paper) to obtain a crude extract, which was characterized by FTIR spectroscopy (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two) to verify functional groups such as hydroxyl and aromatic bonds. The extract was subsequently oven-dried (Memmert UN55, 60 °C, 24 h) and centrifuged (Hettich Rotina 380, 5000 rpm, 15 min) to yield purified inhibitor powder, stored at ambient temperature.

Protective smart coatings were formulated with epoxy resin as the matrix. Per batch, 0.3 g silica fume nanoparticles (selected from preliminary optimization for improved stability and loading) and lanthanum (as self-healing agent) were pre-washed with 100 mL anhydrous methanol, redispersed in 5 mL methanol, and mixed with 15 g epoxy resin under mechanical stirring (IKA RW 20, 300 rpm, 45 min). Viscosity was adjusted by adding toluene dropwise (5 drops every 10 min). For turmeric variants, 0.2 g purified extract was incorporated. Formulations included: E (pure epoxy), S (epoxy + silica fume), SL (epoxy + silica fume + lanthanum), ST (epoxy + silica fume + turmeric), and ESLT (epoxy + silica fume + lanthanum + turmeric). Triethylenetetramine hardener was added at a 2:1 resin-to-hardener ratio, with duplicate batches prepared for consistency.

The mixtures were spin-coated (Chemat KW-4A) onto prepared St37 substrates (Section 2.1) to a uniform 150 μm thickness (verified by Elcometer 456). Samples were dust-protected during initial ambient curing (7 days), followed by post-curing at 80 °C for 2.5 h to optimize cross-linking and integrity.

The turmeric extract-to-silica fume ratio in the ESLT formulation was optimized at approximately 4:1 (0.2 g turmeric extract to 0.3 g silica fume per batch), determined from preliminary screening tests evaluating dispersion stability and corrosion inhibition efficiency. This ratio ensured effective loading of active organic compounds within the mesoporous silica fume structure while maintaining homogeneous mixing and synergistic interaction with lanthanum ions. Higher ratios compromised dispersion, whereas lower ratios reduced inhibitor availability, confirming the selected proportion as optimal for achieving the reported ∼98% inhibition efficiency.

Microcapsules containing lanthanum (La), silica fume, and turmeric extract were synthesized using a co-precipitation method to enhance the self-healing properties of the coatings. The encapsulation process was performed separately for each inhibitor (lanthanum and turmeric) and as a combined mixture, with silica fume serving as the carrier matrix.

For lanthanum encapsulation, 0.1 g of lanthanum in 50 mL of distilled water in 100 mL beakers. These solutions were stirred using magnetic stirrers at 300 rpm for 15 min at room temperature. The lanthanum solution required no pH adjustment due to its neutral behavior in aqueous media. For silica fume, 0.5 g was dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water, and the pH was adjusted to 6.2 optimal for silica stability using dilute solutions of 1 M HCl and 4 M NaOH. These solutions were prepared by adding 10 drops of each acid or base to 20 mL of distilled water, with pH monitored using a pH meter. The silica fume suspension was stirred for 10 min to ensure homogeneity. Subsequently, the lanthanum and silica fume solutions were combined, and 100 mL of the mixture was transferred to a vacuum flask (Model Buchi V-100) and subjected to vacuum treatment at 0.1 bar for 1 h to remove air bubbles and enhance encapsulation. The resulting suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 2 min, washed twice with 50 mL distilled water, and centrifuged again under the same conditions to isolate the microcapsules.

Turmeric extract microcapsules were prepared similarly, with a turmeric-to-silica fume ratio of 10:1 (e.g., 0.5 g turmeric extract to 0.05 g silica fume). The turmeric extract, obtained as described in Section 2.2, was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and mixed with the silica fume suspension under magnetic stirring (300 rpm, 15 min). The pH was adjusted to 6.2 using the same dilute HCl and NaOH solutions, followed by vacuum treatment (0.1 bar, 1 h) in a vacuum flask. The suspension was then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 2 min, washed twice with 50 mL distilled water, and centrifuged again to collect the microcapsules.

For the combined lanthanum and turmeric inhibitor system, 0.52 g of lanthanum was first dissolved in 50 mL distilled water, stirred for 15 min, and allowed to precipitate. Without washing (to retain a higher lanthanum concentration), 50 mL of a turmeric solution (0.5 g turmeric extract) was added. The mixture was transferred to an Erlenmeyer flask, combined with 0.05 g silica fume, and subjected to vacuum treatment (0.1 bar, 1 h). The resulting suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 2 min, washed twice with 50 mL distilled water, and centrifuged again to obtain the dual-inhibitor microcapsules.

The collected microcapsules from all batches were spread on watch glasses and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 24 h to remove residual moisture. The dried microcapsules were gently ground using a mortar and pestle to yield a free-flowing powder, suitable for incorporation into the coating formulations.

The nominal (theoretical) inhibitor loadings, based on input masses, were approximately 51 wt% lanthanum for the La-only system (0.52 g La/(0.52 g La +0.5 g silica fume)), ∼91 wt% turmeric for the turmeric-only system (0.5 g turmeric/(0.5 g turmeric + 0.05 g silica fume)), and ∼95 wt% total inhibitors for the dual system (1.02 g inhibitors/(1.02 g + 0.05 g silica fume)). Considering typical encapsulation efficiencies of 70–85% for co-precipitation on mesoporous silica fume, the actual loaded contents are estimated at 35–43 wt% La, 64–77 wt% turmeric, and 67–81 wt% combined inhibitors, respectively. These high loadings contributed to the effective release and superior self-healing performance observed.”

The corrosion resistance of coated St37 steel samples was assessed using salt spray testing, EIS, and PDP measurements. For salt spray testing (conducted according to ASTM G85, with parameters consistent with ASTM B117 for neutral salt spray), two 2 × 2 cm squares were marked on each coated plate, and X-cut scratches were introduced using a surgical blade to evaluate self-healing performance. The scratched samples were allowed to heal at room temperature (25 °C) for 24 h. Subsequently, all areas except the scratched squares were sealed with beeswax, and the samples were exposed to a continuous 3.5 wt% NaCl fog at 35 °C in a salt spray chamber (Model Q-Fog CRH600) for 100 h. Visual inspections were conducted at 50-h intervals: plates were removed, rinsed with distilled water, air-dried, and photographed to document corrosion progression and self-healing efficiency [20].

Electrochemical tests were performed to investigate the corrosion behavior of uncoated and coated St37 steel in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. All measurements were conducted using an OrigaFlex OGF500 Potentiostat/Galvanostat system. A three-electrode configuration was employed, comprising the steel sample as the working electrode (exposed area: 1 cm2), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a platinum electrode as the counter electrode. EIS measurements were carried out over a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz, with a sinusoidal amplitude of ±10 mV relative to the open-circuit potential (Eocp), selected to ensure a linear response while minimizing electrode perturbation. PDP curves were obtained by scanning the potential from −200 mV to +200 mV versus Eocp at a scan rate of 0.5 mV/s, enabling the determination of corrosion current density and rate. All tests were performed in triplicate to ensure reproducibility [21,22].

The chemical composition and structural properties of the powdered microcapsules were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy (Model Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10) over a wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1 and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Model Bruker D8 Advance) with Cu Kα radiation to confirm phase purity and crystallinity. The microstructural features of the coatings and microcapsules were examined using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (Model Zeiss Sigma 300) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV.

To analyze the chemical bonds and functional groups within the microcapsules, FTIR spectroscopy was utilized. The results of this study are shown in Fig. 2 . The infrared spectrum for turmeric indicates the presence of phenolic compounds such as curcumin and its derivatives. Characteristic bands in different regions of the spectrum indicate various functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and alkynes, each of which plays an important role in the chemical properties of turmeric. A broad band in the range of 3600-3200 cm−1 indicates hydroxyl groups. A strong band in the range of 1600-1700 cm−1 is usually related to carbonyl stretching, which in curcumin can correspond to the ketone group [23]. The absorption peaks observed between 1600 and 1500 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of (C=C) double bonds in aromatic carbon rings, which are characteristic of curcumin and other phenolic components found in turmeric [24].The absorption bands in the range of 1300-1000 cm−1 indicate stretching. Carbon-oxygen bonds can be related to phenolic, alcoholic, or ether groups [25]. The strong and broad absorption peak at 3433.06 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H or N–H groups, which are characteristic of alcohol and amine compounds present in turmeric extract [26].

The C-H stretching vibration peak, associated with both aliphatic and aromatic groups, was observed at a wavelength of 2927.74 cm−1 [27]. At 1647.10 cm−1, the peak corresponds to the C=C stretching vibrations in aromatic rings [28]. The absorption peaks at 1508.23 cm−1 are associated with C-C bonds, while those at 1425.30 cm−1 correspond to C-N bonds [29]. Furthermore, the peak at 1080.06 cm−1 indicates the presence of the C-O functional group [30]. The absorption bands at 1027.99 cm−1 signify the presence of C-N bonds [31]. The wavelength 574.75 cm−1 corresponds to the C-H functional group [32]. The wavelengths 1155.28 cm−1 and 1114.78 cm−1 correspond to the C-O functional groups, respectively [33].

The crystallographic structure and phase composition of the coating composites, comprising SL and SLT microcapsules, were analyzed using XRD, with results depicted in Fig. 3 . The XRD patterns were acquired using a diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 A°) at an operating voltage of 40 kV and a current of 30 mA, scanned over a 2θ range of 10° to 80° with a step size of 0.02° and a scan rate of 2°/min [34,35].

Fig. 3 displays distinct diffraction patterns for the SL and SLT Coatings. The SL sample exhibits prominent peaks at approximately 22°, corresponding to the amorphous silica (SiO2) phase and at 28°, attributed to crystalline lanthanum or lanthanum oxide (La2O3, JCPDS No. 04-006-0625). Minor peaks at around 30° and 60° indicate the presence of chloride (Cl) and sodium (Na) impurities, likely originating from synthesis reagents such as HCl and NaOH. In the SLT pattern, the peaks are broader and less intense, particularly around 22°– 28°, reflecting the incorporation of turmeric extract. This broadening suggests the introduction of organic phases from turmeric, which reduce the crystallinity of the SL matrix and may enhance the Coating's flexibility [36,37].

The XRD results validate the effective encapsulation of lanthanum within the silica matrix, as evidenced by the La-related peaks, and highlight the structural modification induced by turmeric in the SLT Coating. The reduced crystallinity in SLT, indicated by peak broadening, is likely to enhance the self-healing and corrosion-resistant properties of the coating by improving its adaptability to mechanical stress in a 3.5 wt% NaCl environment.

FTIR and XRD analyses were primarily conducted on the inhibitor-loaded microcapsules (silica fume, lanthanum, and mixed silica fume + lanthanum + turmeric systems) to confirm encapsulation efficiency and chemical integrity. The spectra showed characteristic Si–O–Si bands from silica fume, preserved organic functional groups (O–H ∼3400 cm−1, C=O ∼1630 cm−1, aromatic C=C ∼1500–1600 cm−1) from turmeric extract, and lanthanum-related features. No evidence of chemical degradation or unwanted reactions was observed, supporting effective loading.

XRD patterns of the loaded nanoparticles primarily confirmed the amorphous character of silica fume (broad diffraction hump centered at ∼22° 2θ) and the retention of crystalline lanthanum phases (characteristic peaks corresponding to La2O3 and/or La(OH)3). No additional crystalline phases were detected in the mixed loaded systems, indicating structural stability and absence of undesired reactions during inhibitor incorporation. These results support phase preservation rather than providing elemental composition data, which was separately assessed via EDS.

The patterns confirmed the amorphous nature of silica fume (broad hump ∼22° 2θ) and retention of crystalline lanthanum phases (peaks consistent with La2O3/La(OH)3). The turmeric-only loaded system (ST) exhibited no additional crystalline peaks beyond the amorphous silica hump, as expected for organic-rich amorphous materials; thus, its pattern was omitted to avoid redundancy (FTIR was used instead for organic component verification). Minor low-intensity peaks near ∼30° and ∼60° in SL and SLT are attributed to trace NaCl impurities (from pH adjustment reagents HCl and NaOH), remaining after washing. These traces are negligible and do not indicate significant contamination or interference with inhibitor functionality.

Among the key methods for assessing the corrosion resistance of protective coatings is the salt spray test. This test is particularly important for smart coatings based on epoxy resin, which are used in various industries including automotive, marine, and construction [38]. The salt spray test can help optimize coatings to enhance corrosion resistance. The results of the salt spray test for the 5 codes defined in the study, are presented in Fig. 4 . As shown in the figure, the test was conducted in two 50-h intervals, and qualitative photos were taken after each stage. The abbreviations for the codes Coating are as follows:
Code 1: Epoxy (E), Code 2: Epoxy-Silica (ES), Code 3: Epoxy-Silica-Lanthanum (ESL), Code4: Epoxy-Silica-Turmeric (EST), Code5: Epoxy-Silica-Lanthanum-Turmeric (ESLT).

As shown in Fig. 4, the initial epoxy resin coating exhibits relatively low corrosion resistance. However, in the case of the epoxy coating combined with nano silica fume particles, the lanthanum mineral inhibitor, and the green turmeric plant inhibitor, the effect of the inhibitors in the coating is clearly observable, and they have shown good corrosion resistance. All the analytical and qualitative results from this test can be observed in the scratched section.

The corrosion morphology in scratched areas after 100 h of salt spray exposure (Fig. 4) provides clear evidence of effective self-healing, especially for the ESLT coating. While physical scratch marks remained visible (as expected for inhibitor-release rather than polymeric reflow mechanisms), corrosion was dramatically suppressed: only minor rust traces appeared in ESLT versus widespread red rust in E and intermediate coatings. This, combined with sustained high EIS impedance, recovered Rct values, and lower O/Cl content from EDS (Table 1 ) in scratched regions, confirms active release of lanthanum and turmeric inhibitors, protective film formation, and strong self-healing performance.

The microstructural morphology and self-healing behavior of scratched coating samples, were evaluated using FE-SEM. Images were captured using an FE-SEM system at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV and a working distance of approximately 10 mm [39], providing high-resolution details of the scratched areas after 100 h of exposure to a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. FE-SEM micrographs (Fig. 5 ) illustrate the progressive self-healing performance across the samples. Sample a(E) exhibits a smooth epoxy surface with a prominent, unhealed scratch, indicating significant corrosion damage and no self-repair capability. Sample b(ES) shows partial filling of the scratch with silica fume particles, suggesting initial mechanical reinforcement but limited healing. Sample c(ESL) displays a moderately healed scratch, with lanthanum deposits partially sealing the damaged area, evidencing early self-healing induced by lanthanum. Sample d(EST) reveals a more filled scratch, with a flexible organic layer from turmeric extract contributing to enhanced repair and surface adaptability. Sample e(ESLT) demonstrates the most effective healing, with the scratch nearly closed due to the synergistic action of lanthanum and turmeric, forming a dense, protective layer. Cross-sectional views confirm a uniform coating thickness of approximately 150 μm across all samples, with microcapsules embedded throughout, supporting their role in corrosion resistance. Minor pores or cracks, likely from the curing process, were observed but remained negligible, reinforcing the coatings’ structural integrity.

The elemental composition and corrosion resistance of the scratched areas were further investigated using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), with results presented in Fig. 6 . The EDS analysis was performed using an integrated system coupled with the FE-SEM, operating under the same conditions (15 kV), to map elemental distributions across samples a(E), b(ES), c(ESL), d(EST), and e(ESLT) after 100 h of exposure to a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

The EDS spectra (Fig. 6) reveal distinct elemental profiles. For sample a(E), the spectrum is dominated by carbon (C) and oxygen (O) peaks from the epoxy matrix, with minor sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl) peaks indicating surface corrosion from the saline environment. Sample b(ES) shows additional silicon (Si) peaks from silica fume [40], alongside increased Na and Cl, suggesting greater salt penetration. In sample c(ESL), lanthanum (La) peaks emerge alongside Si, C, and O, with a reduced Cl intensity, reflecting lanthanum's role in mitigating corrosion through self-healing. Sample d(EST) exhibits prominent C and O peaks, with potential nitrogen (N) or sulfur (S) traces from turmeric's organic compounds, and a decreased Na and Cl presence, indicating a protective organic layer. Sample e(ESLT) displays a complex spectrum with La, Si, C, O, and minor N/S peaks, alongside a significant reduction in Na and Cl, confirming the synergistic protective effect of lanthanum and turmeric, which enhances the coating's corrosion resistance and self-healing capacity. The results (Table 1) reveal higher oxygen and chlorine contents in the pure epoxy (E) and less inhibited coatings, indicating more extensive corrosion product accumulation (e.g., iron oxides/hydroxides and chlorides). In contrast, the ESLT coating exhibited significantly lower O and Cl levels, along with detectable La and enriched C, consistent with effective inhibitor adsorption, protective film formation, and superior self-healing performance.

The composite fillers were prepared by in-situ loading of lanthanum and/or turmeric extract onto mesoporous silica fume nanoparticles via co-precipitation, resulting in sub-micron loaded particles rather than conventional core-shell microcapsules. Although dedicated SEM/TEM imaging of the isolated loaded nanoparticles was not conducted, the successful encapsulation and release were confirmed indirectly through FTIR, XRD, and superior self-healing performance in scratched coatings.

In this section, electrochemical studies are presented as one of the key tools for evaluating the performance of anti-corrosion coatings. These methods allow for the examination of the resistance of coatings against electrochemical reactions, thereby enabling the evaluation of their protective efficacy in corrosive environments. Electrochemical studies are particularly useful for determining important parameters such as corrosion current density, corrosion potential, and polarization resistance [41,42]. In this study, various electrochemical techniques, including Tafel Polarization and EIS, were employed. Tafel polarization tests were conducted to determine the corrosion current density and corrosion potential of the coatings. Conversely, the EIS method was employed to study the impedance characteristics of the coatings and assess the charge transfer resistance and surface resistance. These tests were carried out in a standard corrosive environment (3.5 wt% NaCl) and under controlled temperature and time conditions. The polarization technique is commonly utilized to assess the protective capabilities of substrates. In this study, uncoated epoxy carbon steel samples were exposed to a 3.5 wt% NaCl environment in the presence of loads prepared with a ratio of 0.1 gr of silica fume along with the mineral inhibitor lanthanum and the green inhibitor turmeric plant, which had been mixed for 24 h. Polarization tests were conducted, and the results are presented in Table 2 . The results of the tests on samples coated with epoxy resin along with 0.3 g of silica fume, lanthanum, and the green inhibitor from turmeric, which were immersed for 24 h, are also presented in Table 3 .

As illustrated in Table 2, the combination of silicate fume with the mineral inhibitor lanthanum has the highest inhibition percentage, around 72%. Additionally, the combination of silicate fume with the green inhibitor turmeric plant, with an inhibition percentage of about 30%, is used as a suitable load to prevent steel corrosion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl environment. The high current density of the combination of silica fume with lanthanum and turmeric plant as a mixed compound to prevent steel corrosion was approximately 0.0119 mA/cm2, which did not effectively prevent steel corrosion. When the substrate was coated with an epoxy resin along with nano silica fume particles (0.3 gr), a lanthanum mineral inhibitor, and a green turmeric plant inhibitor (ESLT), the corrosion current density for the steel decreased to 0.0016 mA/cm2, indicating the best coating for preventing carbon steel corrosion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl environment. As seen in Table 3, the inhibition efficiency increased by approximately 98 percent. From Tables 3 and it can be concluded that the coating containing ESLT has the lowest corrosion current density, thus demonstrating the best corrosion resistance. Additionally, the coatings containing EST, ESL, and ES have the lowest corrosion current densities of 0.0038, 0.0048, and 0.0159 mA/cm2, respectively, with inhibition efficiencies of approximately 96%, 97%, and 87%.

EIS was performed to investigate the self-healing behavior of the coatings [43,44]. The Nyquist, Bode, and Phase curves for silica fume compounds with a quantity of 0.1 g, along with the mineral inhibitor lanthanum and the green inhibitor turmeric plant for carbon steel in a 3.5 wt%NaCl environment, are displayed in Fig. 7 (a), (b), and (c), respectively. The parameters obtained from the EIS curves are also presented in Table 4 . As seen in Fig. 7(a), the depressed semicircles indicate that corrosion is under charge transfer control. The SL combination has the largest semicircle diameter, and the ST combination, which includes the green turmeric plant inhibitor, is considered the next combination. All these results are consistent with the polarization section. The semicircular behavior of the curves indicates an equivalent electrical circuit with a resistor and a capacitor [45], as shown in Fig. 7(a). Owing to the non-uniformity of electrode surfaces and the irregular distribution of current and potential caused by electrode geometry, the constant phase element (CPE) is employed to model the ideal capacitor, with its impedance defined by Equation (1) [46],:
(1)
Z
CPE =
1
Y 0
( jw )
n
Z is a constant phase frequency, Y0 is a constant CPE. j is a complex number and ω is the angular frequency. n is a number between −1 and 1.

Regarding the examination of the Bode plots in Fig. 7(b), it is observed that at low frequencies, the absolute value of the impedance increases, indicating an increase in corrosion resistance for the (SL) composite. In Fig. 7(c), the Phase diagrams show greater phase changes at low frequencies for the (SL) composite, all indicating an increase in corrosion resistance of this composite for carbon steel. From Tables 4 and it can be concluded that the combination of silica fume with lanthanum and the green inhibitor from the turmeric plant (SLT) has the lowest charge transfer resistance of approximately 682.9 Ω cm−2, indicating that it will not be a good combination for increasing the steel's resistance. When the steel samples were treated with an epoxy resin coating, nano silica fume particles, a lanthanum mineral inhibitor, and a turmeric plant green inhibitor, the charge transfer resistance increased to 929.8 Ω cm−2, indicating a suitable coating for carbon steel.

The Nyquist plots (Fig. 8 (a)) reveal the impedance response of the coatings, characterized by semicircular arcs whose diameters correspond to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and coating resistance. The epoxy-only coating exhibits a smaller arc diameter, indicating lower corrosion resistance due to direct exposure to the saline environment. The addition of silica fume nanoparticles increases the arc diameter slightly, reflecting improved barrier properties. The incorporation of lanthanum further enlarges the arc, suggesting enhanced inhibition of corrosion through lanthanum's passivation effect. The combination of silica fume, lanthanum, and turmeric results in the largest arc diameter, indicating a synergistic enhancement in corrosion resistance, likely due to the formation of a dense, protective layer that limits chloride ion penetration.

The Bode magnitude plots (Fig. 8(b)) display the impedance modulus (|Z|) as a function of frequency. The epoxy-only coating shows a low |Z| value at low frequencies (e.g., <103 Ω cm2), indicative of poor protective performance. The silica fume-enhanced coating increases |Z| modestly, reflecting a reinforced barrier. The lanthanum-containing coating exhibits a higher |Z| at low frequencies, suggesting effective corrosion inhibition. The ternary system (SLT) achieves the highest |Z| (e.g., >105 Ω cm2), with a lower breakpoint frequency (f0), indicating a thicker and more intact protective layer. This trend underscores the combined effect of inorganic and organic inhibitors in enhancing the coating's impedance.

The phase angle plots (Fig. 8(c)) provide insight into the capacitive behavior of the coatings. The epoxy-only coating shows a phase angle peaking near 60° at mid-frequencies, declining at low frequencies due to water uptake and corrosion initiation. The silica fume addition shifts the peak closer to 70°, indicating improved coating uniformity. The lanthanum-enhanced coating maintains a phase angle near 80° over a broader frequency range, reflecting a more capacitive response and reduced electrolyte penetration. The ternary coating (SLT) exhibits a phase angle approaching 90° at mid-frequencies, with a gradual decline at low frequencies, suggesting a highly uniform and protective film that minimizes corrosive degradation.

The EIS results collectively demonstrate that the integration of silica fume nanoparticles, lanthanum mineral inhibitor, and green turmeric plant inhibitor significantly enhances the corrosion resistance of the epoxy coating on carbon steel. The largest impedance and highest phase angle observed in the ternary system highlight its superior protective capability, attributed to the synergistic barrier and inhibition effects, making it a promising candidate for long-term corrosion protection in saline environments [47].

Table 5 shows the results obtained from EIS diagrams for steel samples coated with epoxy resin combined with silica fume nanoparticles, a lanthanum mineral inhibitor, and a green turmeric plant inhibitor after 24 h of immersion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl environment. As seen, the samples ESL, EST, ES, and Epoxy have charge transfer resistances of approximately 908.6, 698.9, 664.9, and 594.6 Ω cm−2, respectively.

EIS analysis after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl revealed markedly superior anticorrosion performance for the composite coatings compared to pure epoxy. Nyquist plots showed significantly larger capacitive loops, with Rct increasing from 594.6 Ω cm2 (Epoxy) to 929.8 Ω cm2 (Epoxy@Si@La@Tu). Bode plots confirmed higher low-frequency impedance (|Z|{0.01 Hz} > 105 Ω cm2) and broader phase angles near −90° for the loaded systems, demonstrating excellent barrier properties and active inhibition.

The minor differences in inhibition efficiency ranking between direct exposure (uncoated steel) and embedded (coated steel) tests stem from the change in protection mechanism: free adsorption in solution versus controlled release within the epoxy barrier matrix, where polymer-inhibitor interactions modulate synergistic effects.

Although initial electrochemical tests were conducted after 24 h immersion for mechanistic comparison, the 100-h salt spray exposure provided robust evidence of long-term corrosion resistance, with the ESLT coating maintaining excellent barrier and self-healing properties throughout the extended test period.

The electrochemical behavior of smart coatings, comprising epoxy resin, nano silica fume particles, lanthanum, and green turmeric plant inhibitor, was rigorously evaluated for superior corrosion protection of carbon steel. In Fig. 9 (a), the ES coating exhibits corrosion progression through ion diffusion and electron transfer, yielding FeCl2 and Fe(OH)2 via the reactions: O2 + 2H2O + 4e− → 4OH−, Fe → Fe2+ + 2e−, and Fe2+ + 2Cl- → FeCl2, indicating a baseline protective mechanism. Fig. 9(b) reveals the enhanced efficacy of the ESL coating, where La3+ + 3OH− → La(OH)3 and Fe2+ + 2OH− → Fe(OH)2 reactions substantially mitigate corrosion, showcasing a marked improvement in durability. Fig. 9(c) underscores the exceptional performance of the ESLT coating, where curcumin-iron complexes and curcuminis actio protectiva synergistically obstruct electron transfer and deliver robust, long-term stabilization against corrosive environments [48,49].

The superior corrosion resistance of the smart coatings arises from a multi-layered synergistic mechanism involving barrier enhancement, active inhibition, and self-healing. Nano silica fume particles act primarily as a reinforcing filler and mesoporous carrier, improving coating compactness, reducing porosity, and facilitating controlled release of loaded inhibitors upon damage or ion ingress. Lanthanum ions function as an efficient cathodic inhibitor: upon release, La3+ reacts with OH− at cathodic sites to form insoluble La(OH)3 precipitates (Fig. 9(b)), suppressing oxygen reduction and blocking corrosive species penetration. The green turmeric extract, rich in curcuminoids, serves as a mixed-type organic inhibitor: its polar functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, aromatic) adsorb onto the steel surface, forming stable curcumin-iron chelate complexes that passivate both anodic and cathodic sites (Fig. 9(c)). In the combined ESLT system, these mechanisms synergize — silica fume enables sustained dual-inhibitor release, lanthanum provides precipitative sealing, and turmeric offers adsorptive film formation — resulting in exceptional barrier restoration, minimal corrosion product accumulation (confirmed by EDS), and the highest inhibition efficiency (∼98%). This eco-friendly synergy accounts for the outstanding long-term protection observed in EIS, PDP, and salt spray tests.”

This research aims to present a smart coating based on epoxy resin, using silica fume nanoparticles, lanthanum, and turmeric plant extract, aimed at boosting the corrosion resistance and mechanical durability of carbon steel. The findings from EIS and PDP tests demonstrate that the combination of these materials has substantially enhanced the corrosion resistance of steel samples in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The presence of silica fume nanoparticles as a filler and lanthanum as an inhibitor led to improved mechanical performance and prevention of corrosive agent penetration. Moreover, the application of the green inhibitor derived from turmeric demonstrated its potential as an eco-friendly and sustainable alternative to synthetic compounds for corrosion protection.The results obtained from the polarization test for the SL, ST, and SLT compounds in a 3.5 wt%NaCl environment for uncoated steel samples showed that the SL compound can effectively prevent steel corrosion in this environment. Additionally, the results of this test for epoxy-coated steel samples (E, ES, ESL, EST, and ESLT) showed that the ESLT coated sample had the highest inhibition percentage, approximately 98%. From the results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, it can be concluded that for uncoated samples, the SL composition has the highest corrosion resistance for carbon steel, which is consistent with the polarization results. It was also observed from the results that for the coated samples, the ESLT sample has the highest charge transfer resistance, which acts as the best coating on carbon steel in a 3.5 wt% NaCl environment and shows good corrosion resistance.

In general, this research proposes an innovative and sustainable approach to protecting carbon steel in industrial environments, which can help reduce corrosion-related costs and extend the lifespan of equipment. This method is not only technically effective, but also environmentally friendly due to the use of green inhibitors and biodegradable materials.
AmirHossein Hafazeh: Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Hossein Tavakoli: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fatemeh Zahra Hajiyan: Formal analysis, Methodology.
